solution: For a ratio of polynomials of equal degree, the limit is the ratio of the leading coefficients: \( \lim_{n\to\infty} a_n = \dfrac{[[a]]}{[[d]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio of leading coefficients; the limit is (numerator leading)/(denominator leading).
This takes the ratio of the constant terms, which are negligible as \( n \to \infty \).
The degrees are equal, so the limit is not \( 0 \); it would be \( 0 \) only if the numerator had smaller degree.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by two from the correct limit.

solution: The numerator has degree \( 1 \) but the denominator has degree \( 2 \), so the denominator grows faster and \( \lim_{n\to\infty} a_n = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This treats the degrees as equal and takes the leading-coefficient ratio; here the denominator's degree is strictly larger.
This takes the ratio of the constant terms, which is not how the limit behaves at infinity.
The sequence tends to \( 0 \), not \( 1 \), since the denominator dominates.
This reports the numerator's leading coefficient instead of the actual limit \( 0 \).
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This divides by \( A \) directly instead of by \( 2\sqrt{A} \), forgetting the conjugate multiplies both square-root terms.
This wrongly assumes the two square roots cancel exactly to give \( 0 \); they only agree in their leading behavior.
This adds \( B \) and \( E \) instead of subtracting them.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by two from the correct limit.

solution: Divide numerator and denominator by \( n \): the denominator \( \sqrt{[[c]]n^{2}+[[d]]n+[[e]]}/n \to \sqrt{[[c]]} \), so \( a_n \to \dfrac{[[a]]}{\sqrt{[[c]]}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This divides by \( c \) directly rather than by \( \sqrt{c} \), forgetting the square root in the denominator.
This multiplies by \( \sqrt{c} \) instead of dividing by it.
This takes the ratio of the constant terms, which does not determine the limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by two from the correct limit.
